quick_facts.label: Credential and route
quick_facts.value: A Ph.D. in Engineering is a research doctorate. GATE is one recognised national-test/fellowship route that higher-education institutions (HEIs) may use for Ph.D. admission and interview-based selection; it does not by itself guarantee admission or make GATE mandatory at every institution.

quick_facts.label: National duration standard
quick_facts.value: Under the UGC Ph.D. Regulations, 2022, the minimum duration is 3 years including coursework and the normal maximum is 6 years from admission. An HEI may permit up to 2 further years through re-registration, subject to the regulation’s overall limits.

quick_facts.label: Coursework baseline
quick_facts.value: UGC requires at least 12 Ph.D. coursework credits, including research methodology and Research and Publication Ethics. Scholars must earn at least 55% or an equivalent grade in coursework to continue and submit the thesis.

quick_facts.label: Typical direct-entry pattern
quick_facts.value: Direct doctoral entry after a B.E./B.Tech. is available at some engineering institutions for candidates with a valid GATE score, subject to discipline-specific academic eligibility and selection. For example, IISc’s 2026 Computer Science and Automation Ph.D. information lists a valid GATE score as one qualifying route for eligible B.Tech.-level applicants.

quick_facts.label: Funding and fees are institution-specific
quick_facts.value: For 2026 entry, IIT Delhi lists ₹7,500 tuition per semester for full-time Ph.D. students, while IISc’s AY 2026–27 circular lists annual Ph.D. tuition and other annual fees totalling ₹25,800 for General/EWS/OBC students before refundable deposits; hostel and living costs are separate where applicable.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: At the national minimum-standard level, a candidate can seek Ph.D. admission after a qualifying master’s degree with at least 55% aggregate marks (or equivalent grade), or after a four-year/eight-semester bachelor’s degree with at least 75% aggregate marks (or equivalent). The UGC Regulations allow a 5-percentage-point/equivalent-grade relaxation for specified categories, including SC/ST/OBC (non-creamy layer), Persons with Disabilities and EWS, as decided by the Commission.

For a GATE-supported engineering route, the admitting department’s rules are decisive. IISc’s research-programme admissions information illustrates one common model: candidates with a bachelor’s degree in engineering/technology require a valid GATE score or NET-JRF for that entry category, while a master’s degree in engineering/technology is listed with GATE/NET-JRF/GPAT as desirable. Departmental subject prerequisites, score validity, category rules and shortlisting thresholds can differ by institution and admission cycle.
bullets: Check the qualifying degree, minimum marks/CGPA, accepted GATE paper and score-validity rule in the target department’s current admission notice.
Treat a GATE qualification as an admission/funding pathway rather than an automatic offer of admission.

Applicants from a four-year engineering degree may be eligible for direct Ph.D. entry at institutions that offer it; some institutions also provide exemptions or alternate national-test routes for specified high-CGPA graduates.

sections.heading: Duration
paragraphs: The UGC’s nationwide minimum standard is three years, inclusive of coursework, and the normal maximum is six years from the date of admission. Re-registration can allow up to two additional years under the HEI’s rules; the regulations also provide additional time provisions for women scholars and scholars with more than 40% disability.

Actual completion time in engineering is often longer than the minimum because it depends on prior preparation, coursework, comprehensive examinations, research complexity, publication/thesis requirements and evaluation. As a dated institutional example, IISc’s Computer Science and Automation department describes a typical Ph.D. duration of five to six years.
bullets: Use the statutory 3–6 year range for baseline planning.
Confirm the department’s milestones and maximum-registration rule before accepting an offer.
Do not assume that direct entry after B.Tech. has the same timeline or coursework load as entry after M.Tech.

sections.heading: Curriculum and research milestones
paragraphs: This degree is principally a supervised original-research programme, not a taught engineering course. UGC requires a minimum of 12 coursework credits, including research methodology and Research and Publication Ethics; the Research Advisory Committee may recommend additional relevant courses, including UGC-recognised online courses. Scholars must also receive training in teaching, education, pedagogy or writing related to their doctoral subject.

After coursework, the Research Advisory Committee reviews and finalises the research topic, advises on study design and methodology, and reviews progress. The scholar presents progress each semester. Before thesis submission, the scholar makes a pre-submission presentation; the thesis is subject to plagiarism checking and is evaluated by the supervisor and at least two external experts.

Coursework and qualifying milestones vary materially by department and entry qualification. For example, IISc CSA’s 2026 programme overview expects at least 12 credits for M.Tech.-background entrants and at least 24 credits for B.Tech.-background entrants, followed by a comprehensive examination and research proposal before the main thesis research phase.
bullets: Core requirements: research methodology, research/publication ethics, discipline-relevant advanced study and thesis research.

Typical milestones: supervisor/RAC oversight, coursework completion, comprehensive or qualifying examination where prescribed, research proposal, recurring progress reviews, thesis, external evaluation and viva/defence.
Teaching or research assistance may be assigned; UGC says scholars may be assigned 4–6 hours per week for tutorials, laboratory work or evaluation.

sections.heading: Admission through GATE-supported pathways
paragraphs: The usual process is to identify a department and research area, submit the institute’s online Ph.D. application during its notified cycle, meet its academic and national-test requirements, and clear its selection process. Under the UGC Regulations, an HEI may admit candidates who qualify for fellowships/scholarships in GATE and similar national-level tests through an interview. An HEI may alternatively or additionally use its own entrance test; where it does, the UGC specifies a 70% entrance-test and 30% interview/viva-voce weighting for that route.

Institutions must publish the number of seats, discipline-wise distribution, admission criteria and procedure in their prospectus, and follow the applicable national or state reservation policy. Thus, a candidate should use the current notice rather than relying on a prior year’s cut-off, deadline or score expectation.

A concrete engineering example is IISc: its research admissions information states that B.E./B.Tech. holders with a GATE score who satisfy the relevant engineering department’s basic qualifications can be considered for both Ph.D. and M.Tech. (Research), with the choice communicated to the department at interview.
bullets: Shortlist departments by faculty research fit, laboratories and currently advertised research areas.
Apply to the institution directly; GATE is not a single common-counselling admission offer for all engineering Ph.D. seats.
Prepare for a technical/research interview and, where requested, a research proposal, statement of purpose, transcripts, GATE record and references.
Verify whether the offer is full-time, part-time, sponsored or externally registered, because eligibility, employer approvals, fees and financial support differ.

sections.heading: Costs and financial support
paragraphs: Fees and funding are highly variable by institution, student category, registration mode, scholarship status and accommodation choice. For dated 2026-entry context, IIT Delhi’s 2026–27 prospectus lists full-time Ph.D. tuition of ₹7,500 per semester and part-time/sponsored Ph.D. tuition of ₹17,500 per semester; other charges and living costs may apply.

For another dated 2026–27 example, IISc’s fee circular effective 1 August 2026 lists annual Ph.D. tuition of ₹20,000 plus gymkhana, other academic and student-emergency charges, for an annual total of ₹25,800 excluding refundable deposits for General/EWS/OBC students. The same circular lists Ph.D. tuition as fully waived for SC/ST/PwD students, with the listed annual non-tuition charges bringing the total to ₹5,800 before refundable deposits.

Financial assistance should be checked in the individual admission notice and offer terms. IIT Delhi states that its full-time Institute Assistantship includes teaching/research assistance and, on its current admissions page, lists ₹37,000 per month plus applicable HRA for the first two years and ₹42,000 per month plus applicable HRA thereafter, up to five years. These are institutional figures, not a nationwide guaranteed stipend.
bullets: Budget separately for tuition/academic fees, refundable deposits, hostel or rent, mess/food, health insurance where required, research travel and personal expenses.
Confirm whether a GATE-linked or institute assistantship is attached to the admission category and whether it requires teaching/research duties.
Use fee and stipend figures only as dated planning context; verify the final amount in the current prospectus and admission offer.
